driver.body: Kepler's device strategy inverts the consumer-electronics playbook. It does not chase new form factors to hit price points or win retail shelf space; it builds the **minimum number of form factors required to place its sensor manifold into the maximum number of high-signal physical contexts** [S-067]. There are exactly four, and they are engineered as one modular platform rather than four separate products — the discipline that lets Devices feed breadth (B) into the moat across mobility, energy, logistics, and payments at once, instead of fragmenting it into four incompatible data streams [S-067].

mechanisms.body:
Every Kepler device is architected around a shared sensor-and-compute core — the **Kepler Sensor Module (KSM)** — with form-factor-specific carrier boards and enclosures wrapped around it [S-501]. The KSM carries the inertial, environmental, and acoustic sensors plus an edge-inference NPU; the carrier adds only the connectivity, power, and human-interface elements a given context actually needs [S-068]. This is the exact inverse of the consumer playbook, where each product is designed bespoke to hit a price point and every generation re-validates its own sensor stack from scratch. Kepler deliberately accepts a slightly higher per-unit cost in exchange for a single validated sensor stack that produces **dimensionally consistent data across every context** — a road-vibration reading taken from a vehicle mount and one taken from a courier's handlebar are directly comparable, because they come from the same silicon and the same calibration [S-068]. That comparability is the entire point of the design rule: it is what makes breadth (B) additive rather than fragmented, and it is the single hardware decision that most directly serves the D6 data platform, which can only fuse the six domains' signals if they arrive pre-aligned.

The manifold expresses itself in four form factors, one per high-signal surface:

- The **Kepler Beacon (KB-1)** is the flagship consumer-facing device — a palm-sized navigation-and-payments companion that rides on a vehicle dash or a courier's bar, detailed in the mockup below [S-068].
- The **Kepler Node (KN-2)** is an embedded industrial module designed to be built directly into D1 vehicles and D2 chargers at the point of manufacture — no enclosure, no screen, pure sensing and telemetry [S-068].
- The **Kepler Tag (KT-3)** is a low-cost adhesive asset-tracker for D3 logistics parcels and courier kit, harvesting shock, tilt, and temperature across the delivery graph [S-068].
- The **Kepler Counter (KC-4)** is a merchant-facing payment-and-sensing terminal that anchors D4 at the physical point of sale [S-068].

Read together, the four are not a product catalogue but four apertures onto the same manifold — each dropping the identical KSM into a different physical context so that the data lands pre-aligned for D6 rather than needing to be reconciled after the fact.

The KB-1's value to the user has to be real, or the device never gets placed and the sensor never samples [S-069]. The interface is therefore designed as a genuinely useful navigation-and-payments surface whose data-harvesting is invisible [S-502]. Every element on the rendered screen is a **cross-domain coupling made visible to the user**: the turn card is D1's routing model rendered locally; the "4 min to charge" prompt and the live bay price are D2 telemetry; "TAP TO PAY $12.40" is the D4 wallet executing a transaction on the device's secure element; and the air-quality and road-surface read-outs are the D5 manifold showing the user a sliver of what it is measuring — enough to feel useful, nowhere near the full sample [S-069].

The governing design principle follows directly [S-503]: give the user one visible reason to keep the device powered and mounted for every invisible stream being collected behind it. That principle inverts the metric that actually matters for the business. A device in a drawer samples nothing, so **retention of the hardware in position — not units sold — is the true north-star metric for Devices** [S-069]. The screen is the price Kepler pays to keep the manifold in the world, mounted and sampling, which is why every visible feature is a working piece of a different domain rather than decoration.
